articles.title: Wells Fargo Provides $46M Refi for SoCal Shopping Center
articles.narrative: Wells Fargo has provided $46 million in fixed-rate, interest-only permanent financing for Rosemead Place, a 336,718-square-foot retail center in Rosemead, CA. The shopping center, anchored by a major retailer and featuring a strong tenant mix, is owned by MPM Partners. The transaction was completed in less than 60 days, despite some near-term lease rollover, reflecting the strong lender appetite for well-located, anchored retail assets.

data_points.notable_features: Anchored by a major retailer and located at the intersection of Rosemead Boulevard and Interstate 10, approximately 10 miles east of Downtown Los Angeles.

articles.market_intelligence: The San Gabriel Valley retail market experienced declining asking rents for at least four consecutive quarters, with the average triple-net asking rate dropping to approximately $2.81 per square foot per month in Q1.

articles.title: Habitat for Humanity to Build 179 Co-op Units in Brooklyn and the Bronx
articles.narrative: Habitat for Humanity is developing two cooperative housing projects totaling 179 units across eight sites in Brooklyn and one building in the Bronx, representing the organization's largest construction pipeline to date. The projects, named Constellation in Brooklyn and Claremont House in the Bronx, are designed to provide affordable homeownership opportunities for working New Yorkers, addressing the "missing middle" demographic. These developments leverage various city, state, and federal subsidy programs, including the Open Door Program and the New Infill Housing Opportunity Program from the New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development, and are supported by recent pro-development reforms and significant affordable housing investments from the city.

data_points.notable_features: The buildings will sit on a 99-year ground lease from the Community Land Trust.
data_points.project_notes: The Constellation development in Brooklyn was expanded from 77 to 108 units, and the Claremont House in the Bronx was expanded from 40 to 71 units. Both expansions were enabled by the City of Yes for Housing Opportunity reforms.

people.name: Sabrina Lippman
people.title: CEO, New York City and Westchester County

articles.financing: Utilizes city, state, and federal programs such as the Open Door Program and the New Infill Housing Opportunity Program from the New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development, and the state’s Homes and Community Renewal program. Additional tax abatements for homeowners through Housing Development Fund Corporation (HDFC) co-ops.

articles.title: Al Adelson of the Berkeley Palm Beach: 5 Questions
articles.narrative: Developer Al Adelson is constructing The Berkeley Palm Beach, a luxury condominium tower in West Palm Beach, FL, with units priced from $2 million to $10 million. This project follows the success of his previous development, The Bristol, which demonstrated strong demand for high-end condos west of the Intracoastal Waterway. Adelson is leveraging new construction materials and technology to offer amenities and finishes comparable to The Bristol, but at a lower cost. The developer notes that significant infrastructure improvements and new institutional developments in the immediate vicinity, including a Vanderbilt University graduate school and a Cleveland Clinic hospital, are enhancing the site's appeal and driving sales momentum.

data_points.address: 500 South Australian Avenue, West Palm Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: Located on Clear Lake, which serves as the water supply for West Palm Beach and Palm Beach Island, ensuring no recreational use. Proximity to a new Vanderbilt University graduate school and a 200-bed Cleveland Clinic hospital.
data_points.project_notes: Units are priced from $2 million to $10 million. Construction has commenced. Fern Street is approved to cross the railroad track to Australian Avenue, improving connectivity to I-95.

articles.title: S3 Capital Lends $101M on Luxury Resort Project Near Orlando
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments secured $101 million in construction financing for the Visions Orlando Resort & Spa, a master-planned luxury resort community in Davenport, FL. S3 Capital provided the loan for the 70-acre project, which will feature single-family homes, townhomes, condos, and condo-hotel units, alongside extensive resident amenities and retail. The project's presale-driven capitalization and strong tourism dynamics in the Orlando market were key factors in the lender's decision.

data_points.address: 7007 Osceola Polk Line Road, Davenport, FL
data_points.notable_features: Master-planned luxury resort community with a 10,000-square-foot clubhouse, fitness center, pool, sports courts, wellness facilities, restaurant, bar, movie theater, and golf simulator.
data_points.project_notes: Phases I, II, III, and V, totaling 460 residential units, have sold out. Phase IV includes 277 condo-hotel units. The community is expected to expand through Phase VIII and will include a 4-acre commercial component.

articles.title: Law Firm Alston & Bird Takes 170K SF at 51 West 52nd Street
articles.narrative: International law firm Alston & Bird has signed a 15-year lease for 169,664 square feet at 51 West 52nd Street in Midtown Manhattan, relocating its offices from 90 Park Avenue. The transaction brings the Harbor Group International-owned trophy property to full occupancy, underscoring the continued strength of New York City's prime office market. The landlord's CEO expressed confidence in the resilience of the market for top-tier assets in desirable locations.

data_points.address: 51 West 52nd Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Trophy office property in Midtown Manhattan.
data_points.project_notes: Alston & Bird is relocating from 90 Park Avenue.

articles.market_intelligence: The average office asking rent in Midtown was $86.55 per square foot in May.

articles.title: Why Midwest Stability Continues to Attract Commercial Real Estate Investors
articles.narrative: The Midwest commercial real estate market continues to attract investors due to its inherent stability, which allows for long-term business planning amidst broader market uncertainty. The region benefits from a balanced supply and demand dynamic, avoiding the oversupply seen in some other markets, and fostering steady rent growth through disciplined management and operational efficiencies. While the office sector faces headwinds in some areas, other property types like multifamily, multi-tenant retail, manufactured housing, and industrial assets are performing well, with industrial potentially benefiting from AI-driven logistics infrastructure. Despite ongoing economic uncertainties, the Midwest's resilience and consistent fundamentals are expected to maintain its appeal for investors seeking long-term wealth preservation.

people.name: Michael Glass
people.title: Executive Managing Director, Chief Revenue Officer – Midwest Division

articles.title: Transwestern Completes 321,104 SF Industrial Project in North Houston
articles.narrative: Transwestern has completed Imperial Crossing, a two-building industrial project totaling 321,104 square feet in North Houston. The development offers flexible space configurations, with one building featuring a cross-dock design and 36-foot clear heights, and the other a front-load design with 32-foot clear heights. The project is designed to accommodate a range of tenant sizes, from full-building users to those needing as little as 35,000 square feet.

data_points.notable_features: Two-building industrial project with cross-dock and front-load configurations, featuring 36-foot and 32-foot clear heights respectively.

articles.title: PCC Acquires 252-Unit Multifamily Complex in San Antonio, Plans Capital Improvements
articles.narrative: Platte Canyon Capital (PCC) has acquired Northwood Apartments, a 252-unit multifamily complex in New Braunfels, northeast of San Antonio. The investment firm plans to undertake capital improvements to the amenity spaces, building exteriors, and parking areas. The property was 70 percent occupied at the time of sale.

data_points.address: 902 N Academy Ave, New Braunfels, TX
data_points.notable_features: Built in phases between 1998 and 2017, offering one- and two-bedroom units.
data_points.project_notes: The seller was the property's original developer.

articles.title: Cushman & Wakefield Negotiates 172,089 SF Office Lease in Irving
articles.narrative: Cushman & Wakefield has brokered a significant 172,089-square-foot office lease in Irving's Las Colinas district. Religious nonprofit organization Mercury One will fully occupy the building at 6655 N. MacArthur Blvd. Orion Properties, the owner, was represented by Cushman & Wakefield, who also represented the tenant in the transaction.

articles.title: Madison’s Momentum: Development, retail and innovation continue to fuel Wisconsin’s capital city
articles.narrative: Madison, WI continues to experience robust commercial real estate activity across multiple sectors, driven by strong economic fundamentals, population growth, and municipal policies encouraging density. Mixed-use developments, particularly in downtown areas, are thriving with over 60 projects in various stages, emphasizing walkability and community spaces. The retail sector is a strong performer with low vacancy rates and positive absorption, attracting national brands and local operators, while industrial real estate has rebounded after a temporary slowdown. Despite elevated construction and borrowing costs, developers remain confident, pushing forward with projects and eyeing major redevelopment opportunities downtown.

articles.title: Lombard’s LSC Demolition adds director of operations
articles.narrative: Lacey McKenzie has been appointed as the Director of Operations at LSC Demolition, a Lombard, Illinois-based company. In her new role, McKenzie will be responsible for executive oversight of operations, supporting strategic growth, and enhancing the company's commitment to safety, quality, and client service. Her extensive background in construction and operations management is expected to drive operational excellence within the organization.

articles.title: Oak Brook’s Inland Real Estate Group makes two key hires
Lee & Associates closes 7,635-square-foot industrial lease in Carrollton
articles.narrative: Lee & Associates Dallas-Fort Worth facilitated a new lease agreement for a 7,635-square-foot industrial property in Carrollton, Texas. The transaction involved a tenant expanding and renewing its commitment at the location, with brokers from Lee & Associates representing the tenant and JSC Realty Investment Services representing the landlord.

data_points.project_notes: This transaction represents a lease expansion and renewal for the tenant.

articles.title: Adolfson & Peterson Construction begins renovations at Chisholm Hall at UTSA
articles.narrative: Adolfson & Peterson Construction has commenced a $6 million renovation project at Chisholm Hall, the largest and oldest residence hall at the University of Texas at San Antonio. The project, slated for completion in July 2026, involves comprehensive upgrades to modernize building systems, enhance resident comfort, and improve efficiency across the 120,860-square-foot facility.

data_points.notable_features: UTSA's largest and oldest residence hall, offering spacious student bedrooms with double and triple-occupancy options.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: University of Texas at San Antonio (UTSA)

articles.title: NY Dirt: Here come the rent freeze lawsuits
articles.narrative: New York City's Rent Guidelines Board has approved a rent freeze for rent-stabilized buildings, fulfilling a campaign promise by Mayor Zohran Mamdani. This decision is expected to trigger legal challenges from landlord groups, who may argue that the freeze constitutes a regulatory taking by depriving them of a reasonable return on investment without just compensation, or that it violates due process by not adequately considering rising operating costs. The outcome of such litigation could significantly impact the regulatory framework for half of the city's rental housing stock.

people.name: Massimo D'Angelo
people.title: Co-chair of the real estate industry team

articles.title: It’s official: New York City is getting a rent freeze
articles.narrative: New York City's Rent Guidelines Board has voted to freeze rents on rent-stabilized apartments for both one-year and two-year leases, a decision that fulfills a campaign promise by Mayor Zohran Mamdani and marks a significant victory for tenant advocates. This unprecedented freeze on two-year leases has drawn strong criticism from landlord groups, who argue it will lead to reduced investment in maintenance and repairs, potentially accelerating the deterioration of the city's housing stock. Legal challenges from landlord organizations are anticipated, with some board members and industry representatives decrying the process as politically motivated rather than fact-based.

data_points.notable_features: Rent freeze applies to rent-stabilized apartments for one-year and two-year leases.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Small Property Owners of New York

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: New York City Rent Guidelines Board

people.name: Massimo D’Angelo
people.title: Co-chair of the real estate industry team

articles.title: Policy Pro: DSA wins could revive REST Act, Council strikes ULURP deals
articles.narrative: The Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) caucus in the New York Legislature is projected to expand, potentially bolstering efforts to pass the Rent Emergency Stabilization for Tenants (REST) Act. This legislation aims to enable municipalities outside New York City to implement rent stabilization, a prospect concerning to landlord groups. The article also reports on the Gotham Organization securing approval for its Monitor Point project in Brooklyn after increasing the affordable housing component to 50% of the units, following negotiations with the City Council. Additionally, the Dewey Clinton Park North project advanced despite some council member opposition.

data_points.notable_features: Waterfront development with 50% of units designated as affordable, including units for seniors and formerly homeless New Yorkers.
data_points.project_notes: The project secured approval after a last-minute deal with the City Council to increase affordable apartments from 40% to 50%.

articles.title: Developer looks to bring almost 300 units to Flushing
articles.narrative: Developer Gary Chen has filed an application with the New York City Department of City Planning for a zoning change to construct a 10-story, 296-unit mixed-use project in Flushing, Queens. The proposed development, which would include 33,000 square feet of commercial space and nearly 200 parking spaces, aims to provide approximately 25 percent affordable housing. Chen assembled the development site, comprising multiple parcels, for a total of $26.5 million in 2024.

data_points.address: 32-02 Linden Place, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Approximately 25% of units would be designated for affordable housing.
data_points.project_notes: The development site includes another property on Linden Place and two industrial buildings on Farrington Street, which were acquired for a total of $26.5 million in 2024.

articles.title: Nassau rent board approves modest increases for regulated apartments
articles.narrative: The Nassau County Rent Guidelines Board has approved modest rent increases for the county's 8,100 rent-stabilized units, setting a 1.5% hike for one-year lease renewals and 2.5% for two-year renewals, effective October 1. This decision reflects a compromise between landlords facing rising operating costs and tenants grappling with inflation, with the approved rates being lower than the previous year's increases. The board's vote highlights the ongoing challenge for rent boards to balance housing affordability with property ownership expenses amidst a 5% inflation rate on Long Island.

data_points.notable_features: Rent-stabilized units in Nassau County.
data_points.project_notes: Rent increases of 1.5% for one-year lease renewals and 2.5% for two-year renewals take effect October 1. These rates are lower than the previous year's increases of 2% for one-year and 3% for two-year leases.

articles.title: Donald Trump Jr, wife put down roots in West Palm Beach
articles.narrative: Donald Trump Jr. and Bettina Anderson have purchased a double lot in West Palm Beach's Prospect Park neighborhood, where they intend to construct an eight-bedroom residence. This acquisition follows their recent marriage and marks their decision to establish roots in the area. The move also signifies a continued expansion of the Trump family's real estate footprint in South Florida.

data_points.notable_features: Double lot intended for an eight-bedroom home, suitable for entertaining and political fundraisers.

articles.title: Waterton buys for $81M, NADG scores $121M loan amid Palm Beach-area apartment shortage
articles.narrative: Waterton Residential acquired The Landings at Pembroke Lake Apartments, a 358-unit multifamily complex in Pembroke Pines, for $80.5 million. Separately, North American Development Group secured a $120.6 million construction loan from Wells Fargo Bank to begin building NUVO Delray Beach, a 476-unit apartment complex in Delray Beach. The Delray Beach project will include 25% affordable housing units and is expected to deliver its first apartments by fall 2027, addressing a shortage of multifamily supply in Palm Beach County.
articles.market_intelligence: Multifamily development in Palm Beach County saw its lowest annual delivery since 2011 last year, leading to demand outpacing supply for the first time since 2021. The development pipeline in Palm Beach County reached 4,600 units in Q1 2026, the highest level since 2023. In Broward County, multifamily construction has slowed, with 6,000 units under construction in Q1 2026, the lowest since 2020, and demand outpaced supply last year for the first time in three years.

articles.title: Miami tees up legal fight over Live Local Act
articles.narrative: Miami officials have unanimously approved a measure to challenge Florida's Live Local Act, a state law offering tax breaks and zoning incentives for affordable housing developments. City leaders argue the act undermines local zoning authority, specifically Miami 21, by allowing high-density projects to bypass established review processes and potentially harm historic neighborhoods. While acknowledging the need for workforce housing, the city attorney has been directed to explore legal options to contest the law, following similar actions by other Florida municipalities.

articles.title: Gatsby lands $100M refi for Palm Beach Gardens offices
articles.narrative: Gatsby Florida secured a $100.4 million refinancing loan for DiVosta Towers, a 220,000-square-foot Class A office campus in Palm Beach Gardens, FL. Cirrus Real Estate Partners provided the three-year, floating-rate loan, which will be used to refinance a previous mortgage. The transaction highlights the increasing demand for suburban office space in Palm Beach County, as companies seek more affordable leases compared to downtown West Palm Beach.

data_points.address: 3825 and 3835 PGA Boulevard, Palm Beach Gardens, FL
data_points.notable_features: Class A office campus consisting of two 11-story buildings.
data_points.project_notes: The property was previously acquired by Gatsby in 2020 for $80 million.

articles.market_intelligence: Palm Beach Gardens office landlords reported 24,400 square feet of positive net absorption and 13,800 square feet of leasing activity in Q1. The vacancy rate in Palm Beach Gardens stood at 8.6 percent, with average asking rents at $52.50 per square foot overall and over $60 per square foot for Class A offices. More companies are looking to move to suburban submarkets in Palm Beach County due to high rents in downtown West Palm Beach, which can exceed $100 per square foot.

articles.title: South Florida’s top construction permits: Week ending June 24, 2026
articles.narrative: The largest new building permit in South Florida for the week ending June 24, 2026, was issued for a $130 million construction project at the Link at Douglas mixed-use development in Miami. This permit is for the fourth and final tower of the project. Additionally, Keiser University secured an $8 million permit for a new commercial building in West Palm Beach, while another $7.05 million commercial permit was issued for a project in West Palm Beach linked to affordable housing and a specialty grocery store. The top residential permit was for seven new homes in PulteGroup's Domaine at SoSo community, with an estimated construction cost of $3.12 million.

data_points.notable_features: Fourth and final tower at a mixed-use development near the Douglas Road Metrorail station.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: LLC tied to Todd Michael Glaser

articles.title: Anagram eyes River North office tower at 75% discount after Blackstone default
articles.narrative: Anagram Real Estate and Glendon Capital Management are reportedly in negotiations to acquire 350 North Orleans Street, a 1.3 million-square-foot office tower in Chicago, out of foreclosure. The potential sale price, estimated between $90 million and $100 million, represents a significant discount from the $378 million paid by the previous owner, Blackstone, in 2015. This transaction highlights the ongoing trend of distressed office asset sales in downtown Chicago, driven by post-pandemic shifts in office demand and higher interest rates.

data_points.notable_features: 13-story high-rise, renovated in 2018, owned separately from an adjacent hotel.
data_points.project_notes: Previous owner Blackstone defaulted on a $310 million loan; the property was previously acquired for $378 million in 2015.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown Chicago office buildings are being purchased at significant discounts, with 500 West Monroe Street selling for a 76 percent discount. Nationally, some office buildings are selling at 90 percent discounts or more, such as the Denver Energy Center, which sold for $5.3 million after a 2013 sale of $176 million.

articles.title: Chicago Housing Authority board power player, tax commissioner jump into mayoral race
articles.narrative: This article reports on two real estate-adjacent figures, Matthew Brewer and George Cardenas, formally entering the race for Chicago mayor. Both candidates bring significant real estate experience, with Brewer having served on the Chicago Housing Authority board and Cardenas as a Cook County Board of Review Commissioner. The city's housing crisis and a substantial construction pipeline logjam are expected to be central issues in the upcoming election.

articles.title: 601W’s bad week gets worse with $43M foreclosure on Tesla-leased Chicago building
articles.narrative: Washington Capital has filed a $42.6 million foreclosure lawsuit against 601W Companies, alleging default on a commercial loan for a Tesla-leased service center at 717 South Desplaines Street in Chicago. The loan, originated in July 2023, matured on May 1, 2026, and was used to finance construction costs for Tesla's 116,000-square-foot service and showroom facility. This foreclosure adds to a series of financial challenges for 601W, including defaults on other significant office property loans in Chicago.

data_points.notable_features: Located near the southwest corner of Chicago's Loop.
data_points.project_notes: 601W venture purchased the 4.5-acre site for $34 million in 2018. The site previously held a vacant warehouse and was eyed for a data center development by a prior owner. The loan provided financing for construction costs tied to the tenant's lease, including a $1.2 million tenant improvement allowance and a $9.3 million approved project budget to be funded by 601W.

articles.title: 1903 Frank Lloyd Wright registered landmark repairs will cost millions
articles.narrative: A Frank Lloyd Wright-designed home on Chicago's West Side, known as the Walser House, is slated for a $3.5 million restoration after falling into disrepair. The property was recently acquired by Austin Coming Together, a nonprofit focused on community development, from Community Initiatives for $125,000. The extensive renovation will address significant damage, including the roof, windows, and moisture issues, with a commitment to preserving the home's original Prairie School design as a Chicago landmark and National Register of Historic Places property.

data_points.notable_features: Frank Lloyd Wright-designed home, Chicago landmark, part of the National Register of Historic Places, built in the Prairie School design style.
data_points.project_notes: The acquisition price included $60,000 from the city's Troubled Buildings Initiative. The building has been vacant since 2019 and requires substantial repairs to the roof, windows, and interior due to moisture damage. The restoration must adhere to the original Prairie School design.

articles.title: Houston Rodeo partially breaks from NRG Park with $300M livestock campus
articles.narrative: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is developing a new 1 million-square-foot livestock complex in Houston, TX, valued at $300 million. This facility, located at Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard, will host various livestock and agricultural events, complementing the main rodeo and concert events that will continue at NRG Park. The expansion is part of the Rodeo's long-term vision, offering more flexible planning and year-round event capabilities.

data_points.notable_features: The complex will include two barns totaling 860,000 square feet, a 140,000-square-foot central barn, an outdoor cattle yard, and a combination auction hall and sales convention center.
data_points.project_notes: This project was initially discussed and mapped out in 2019 but was delayed due to the pandemic. Some operations will remain at NRG Park, and the Rodeo is working on a new lease with the Houston Texans and Harris County.

articles.title: If the NHL comes to Austin or Houston, where would the first puck drop?
articles.narrative: Texas billionaire Dan Friedkin, through his Pursuit Sports platform, is exploring bringing a second NHL franchise to Texas, with potential locations in either Austin or Houston. The article discusses potential venues for a new team, including the H-E-B Center at Cedar Park in Austin, which already hosts the AHL's Texas Stars, and Houston's Toyota Center or NRG Park. The Toyota Center, currently undergoing renovations, could offer a co-tenancy model similar to Dallas's American Airlines Center, which houses both the Mavericks and the Stars.

articles.title: Dallas secures $19M worth of incentives for potential Morgan Stanley move
articles.narrative: The Dallas City Council has approved an $18.5 million incentive package to support Morgan Stanley's potential relocation to Dallas. The financial services firm plans to initially occupy 255,000 square feet in Downtown Dallas before moving into a new 700,000-square-foot building at 2401 McKinney Avenue in Uptown by 2031. This move, which could bring 3,800 employees, is part of a larger $1.3 billion development by Trammell Crow Company, with Morgan Stanley committing $700 million towards its new offices. The project aims to solidify Dallas-Fort Worth as a major financial hub, though Morgan Stanley has not yet formally announced its decision.

data_points.notable_features: New development in Uptown Dallas, part of the 'Y'all Street' financial district.
data_points.project_notes: Morgan Stanley is earmarking $700 million for its new offices. Construction on the new building is set to begin in fall 2026, requiring the demolition of an existing restaurant and gym. Morgan Stanley will temporarily occupy 255,000 square feet at Fountain Place in Downtown Dallas before the Uptown move.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown Dallas is experiencing a decline in tenants, with AT&T vacating 2 million square feet (6% of downtown office space) when it moves to Plano. Uptown Dallas, however, is booming with new businesses and financial institutions, including the upcoming Texas stock exchange.

articles.title: Ryan Seacrest sells Wine Country estate for under $19M after price cuts
articles.narrative: TV host Ryan Seacrest has sold his 40-acre estate in Napa Valley for $18.5 million, following several price reductions from its initial listing of $22 million two years prior. The sprawling property, which includes a 10,750-square-foot main house and guest cottage, is surrounded by vineyards and mountains in St. Helena. Seacrest had acquired the estate in September 2020 for $14 million and undertook renovations in 2024.

data_points.address: 232 North Fork Crystal Springs Road, St. Helena, CA
data_points.notable_features: Gated estate with an 8,700-square-foot main house and guest cottage, surrounded by vineyards and mountains, featuring an infinity pool, bocce court, and 220 Italian olive trees.
data_points.project_notes: Property was revamped in 2024 by Silvia Nobili of Nobili Marqués Arquitectura. Ryan Seacrest previously purchased the property in September 2020 for $14 million.

companies_people.label: SELLER BROKER
companies_people.firm_name: Coldwell Banker Boker of the Valley

articles.title: Who is Buffy Wicks? Meet the legislator dividing California real estate
articles.narrative: California legislator Buffy Wicks has introduced AB 736, a bill that proposes to cap real estate transfer taxes statewide at 1.5 percent for most jurisdictions and 3 percent for cities like San Francisco and Culver City. This legislation aims to stimulate housing development by addressing developer concerns about high transfer taxes, which can hinder the conversion of commercial properties into housing. The bill seeks to find a middle ground between a more aggressive taxpayer-backed measure to cap taxes at 0.11 percent and existing higher rates, particularly impacting San Francisco's 'mansion tax' (Measure ULA) and potentially costing the city $100 million annually. While supported by some major developers, the proposal faces opposition from progressives who argue it limits local government revenue for affordable housing and from realtors concerned it could increase homebuying costs in some general-law cities.

articles.title: Clarion pays $132M for a new manufacturing plant in Fremont leased by Tesla
articles.narrative: Clarion Partners has acquired a newly developed 267,100-square-foot industrial manufacturing facility in Fremont, CA, for $132.3 million. The property, known as Milmont Industrial, was sold by Hines and Oaktree Capital Management, who redeveloped the site after purchasing it in 2021. The facility is fully leased to an electric vehicle manufacturer, underscoring Fremont's growing significance as an industrial and manufacturing hub in the Bay Area.

data_points.notable_features: Features first floor and mezzanine offices, 27 dock-high doors, and 427 parking slots, located near Interstates 880 and 680.
data_points.project_notes: The 14-acre site was previously acquired by the sellers in 2021 for $47 million and redeveloped into a modern manufacturing facility.

articles.market_intelligence: Fremont accounted for 75% of advanced manufacturing leasing activity in Silicon Valley last year. Sterling Organization plans an $18 million renovation of a former Fry's store at 43800 Osgood Road into a 143,800-square-foot industrial building to meet manufacturing demand.

articles.title: Presidio Bay opens the curtains on 17-acre urban village in Menlo Park
articles.narrative: Presidio Bay Ventures has unveiled new renderings for its 17.75-acre urban village development in Menlo Park, which will feature nearly 700 residential units, three office buildings, and retail space. The project, which replaces former federal buildings, is designed to include a mix of housing, offices, shops, restaurants, and public parks, with a focus on warm tones and natural materials. The development is a significant undertaking for the city, contributing substantially to its state-mandated housing goals.

data_points.notable_features: The project includes 43,000 square feet of shops and restaurants, a 10,800-square-foot childcare center, and 1.5 million square feet of parking for over 1,700 cars and 1,200 bicycles. It will also feature a sports court, a two-story pavilion, parks, plazas, and lawns.
data_points.project_notes: The 17.75-acre development replaces 16 former federal buildings, including a 20,000-square-foot office lab. The site was previously acquired by Presidio Bay from the U.S. government for $137 million. Of the 670 residential units, 118 will be designated as affordable.

articles.title: Two more Presidio Heights homes reach $10M range as luxury supply dwindles
articles.narrative: Two luxury homes in San Francisco's Presidio Heights neighborhood recently sold for $9.2 million and $10 million, respectively, highlighting the dwindling supply of high-end housing in the city. The sales reflect the significant wealth being generated by the artificial intelligence industry, attracting affluent buyers to the area. The scarcity of mansions in San Francisco, coupled with anticipated IPOs from major AI companies, is contributing to a competitive luxury market.

data_points.project_notes: Property last sold for $6.8 million in 2021.

articles.title: Multiple Factors Drive MOB Growth
articles.narrative: A recent Cushman & Wakefield report, "Vital Signs," highlights the continued strong momentum in the U.S. medical office building (MOB) sector, driven by robust demand and constrained supply. The report indicates that investors are focusing on the sector's positive fundamentals, leading to sustained high occupancy and upward pressure on rents. Key factors contributing to demand include a 5.8% increase in healthcare spending over the past decade and significant growth in specialties like endocrinology and psychiatry. Supply remains tight due to decreased construction, aging inventory, and competition for viable development sites, with Q1 absorption up 71% year-over-year and occupancy at 92.5%. While the expiration of ACA subsidies may impact some patient volumes, the rapidly aging U.S. population is expected to drive substantial future demand for healthcare services, largely covered by Medicare, ensuring the sector's resilience and attractive investment profile.

people.name: Lorie Damon
people.title: Executive Managing Director, Healthcare Advisory Practice

articles.title: Is CRE Truly an Inflation Hedge?
articles.narrative: A recent analysis by Marcus & Millichap challenges the blanket assumption that commercial real estate (CRE) universally acts as an inflation hedge, revealing that the relationship between rent growth and inflation varies significantly by property type. The study, which examined 25 years of data, found that lease length, contract terms, and demand shifts are key factors influencing inflation pass-through. While office properties showed the weakest correlation due to longer lease terms and limited escalators, retail and industrial sectors demonstrated moderate links. Multifamily, self-storage, and hospitality assets exhibited the strongest resistance to inflation, primarily due to shorter lease terms or daily rate adjustments allowing for more frequent rental resets. Despite these differences, the report concludes that CRE generally offers more inflation resistance than equities, particularly in sustained inflationary periods.

articles.title: Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026: Navigating Capital Flow & Deal Dynamics
articles.narrative: The multifamily sector in the Midwest is experiencing a return of investment competition, driven by sellers adjusting pricing expectations and a steady resurgence of buyer interest. Industry experts at Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026 discussed strategies for navigating capital flows, securing financing, and managing loan maturities. Key insights included the increasing competitiveness for capital in larger deals, the use of high-leverage financing for quick value creation, and the growing acceptance of PACE as a mainstream financing solution to mitigate bank participation risk. Despite market shifts, the fundamentals of the Midwest multifamily market, particularly in Chicago, remain strong.

articles.title: Chicago/Midwest People & Company News, week of June 26, 2026
articles.narrative: Cresa has appointed Julie Northcutt-Dunn as president, markets, a newly created executive role focused on driving growth across the company's market operations. She will be based in Cresa's Chicago headquarters. The Inland Real Estate Group has expanded its institutional asset management platform, Inland Institutional, with the appointment of Jacqueline Dagan as associate general counsel and Dhaval Parikh as head of institutional capital formation. PREMIER Design + Build Group has promoted Stacy Reid to vice president of its new Reno office, marking a significant step in the company's national expansion. JLL has appointed Chris Fernandez to lead its workplace management business across the Americas, overseeing teams and enhancing operational performance. PsiQuantum has hired Josh Koons as vice president of facilities in Chicago, where he will lead the development of the company's facility at the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park. Cushman & Wakefield has added Matt Hiatt as director to its office leasing team in St. Louis. Sartorius's Center of Excellence for Bioanalytics in Ann Arbor, Michigan, has received LEED v4 Gold certification, recognizing its sustainable construction and energy-efficient operations.

articles.title: Report: Has Brexit Been a Boon to the UK?
articles.narrative: Economist Ray Perryman argues that Brexit has severely harmed the UK economy, citing data indicating a significant reduction in GDP by 6% to 8% and a fall in investment by 12% to 18%. He attributes these declines to the uncertainty and disruptions caused by leaving the EU, despite acknowledging other contributing factors like the pandemic and energy policy. Perryman concludes that restricting trade and the flow of capital and labor through Brexit was an unfortunate choice with long-term implications for growth, especially given existing global economic challenges.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

articles.title: Have We Reached The End Of The Bid-Ask Standoff?
articles.narrative: This article discusses the apparent end of the bid-ask standoff in commercial real estate, with institutional capital beginning to deploy more actively. Affinius Capital's $3.4 billion take-private acquisition of Veris Residential is highlighted as a significant transaction signaling this shift. A partner at Affinius Capital noted that the deal represents a change in investment thesis, moving multifamily investment back to its core principles of income production, downside protection, and inflation hedging, rather than tactical, opportunistic plays. He also observed that historical market declines similar to recent years have often been followed by periods of strong investor returns.

articles.title: Federal Board Critiques 'Piecemeal' Approach To Selling D.C. Buildings
articles.narrative: The Public Buildings Reform Board (PBRB) has criticized the federal government's "piecemeal" approach to selling millions of square feet of federal properties in Washington D.C.'s southwest corridor. Board members argue that selling individual properties without a coordinated master plan for the area's future use will not maximize taxpayer returns or attract significant investment. They emphasized the need for a governing entity to lead development planning and secure an anchor tenant to drive demand for the substantial amount of available property. While acknowledging the quick sales of two initial properties, the board believes a comprehensive strategy is essential for the long-term success of the 230-acre federal land disposition.

articles.title: Real Capital Solutions Debuts In Atlanta With $50M Purchase Of 101 Marietta
articles.narrative: Real Capital Solutions has acquired the 36-story, 674,000-square-foot office tower at 101 Marietta Street NW in Downtown Atlanta for $49.5 million, marking the firm's entry into the Atlanta market. The building, known for its distinctive chevron-shaped roof and proximity to Centennial Olympic Park, was purchased at a significant discount compared to its 2015 sale price, reflecting the challenging conditions in Downtown Atlanta's office market. The new owner plans to implement strategic leasing initiatives and build on existing improvements to increase occupancy and enhance the tenant experience.

data_points.notable_features: Iconic skyscraper with a distinctive chevron-shaped roof, located near Centennial Olympic Park.
data_points.project_notes: Formerly known as Centennial Tower. The previous owner, Dilweg Co., had an outstanding mortgage balance of $68.5M as of March 2025, with the loan maturing in August 2025. Dilweg Co. acquired the tower in 2015 for $68.8M with a $64.5M loan from Citizens Bank.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown Atlanta's office market experienced a 30.3% vacancy rate in the first quarter of 2026.

articles.title: REIT's Liquidation Plan Upended After $280M Sale Falls Through
articles.narrative: Elme Communities, a multifamily REIT undergoing liquidation, has withdrawn its shareholder payout estimate after a $280 million deal to sell its largest remaining asset, the 1,222-unit Riverside Apartments in Alexandria, VA, collapsed. The terminated contract, which included adjacent undeveloped land, introduces uncertainty regarding the timing and proceeds of the property's sale, impacting the REIT's ability to fully repay a $520 million term loan from Goldman Sachs. This development has led to a significant drop in Elme's stock value and necessitates renewed marketing efforts for the property.

data_points.project_notes: The terminated contract included adjacent undeveloped land. The REIT is also selling Elme Bethesda, a 193-unit apartment building at 5114 Dudley Lane, Bethesda, MD, for $58 million, down from an initial $59 million.
